content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN. You can do the following operation any number of times:


Select any index iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) and either increase AiA_iAi​ by 111 or decrease AiA_iAi​ by 111.
Find the **minimum** number of operations required to make the MEX of the array maximum possible.

The MEX (minimum excluded) of an array is the smallest non-negative integer that does not belong to the array. For instance:


The MEX of [2,2,1][2,2,1][2,2,1] is 000, because 000 does not belong to the array.
The MEX of [3,1,0,1][3,1,0,1][3,1,0,1] is 222, because 000 and 111 belong to the array, but 222 does not.
The MEX of [0,3,1,2][0,3,1,2][0,3,1,2] is 444 because 000, 111, 222 and 333 belong to the array, but 444 does not.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the number of elements in the array AAA.
The second line of input contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **minimum** number of operations required to make the MEX of the array **maximum** possible.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of operations required to make the MEX of the array as large as possible.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: after sorting [0,0,3,3] we pair with [0,1,2,3] giving |0-0|+|0-1|+|3-2|+|3-3|=2.
Test case 2: array already is a permutation of [0,1,2]; cost 0.
Test case 3: sorted [0,1,4,4] paired with [0,1,2,3] gives cost 3.

solution.approach: To maximise the MEX we need the array to contain every integer from 0 to N‑1 at least once. Because we have exactly N positions, the only way to achieve MEX = N (the maximum possible) is to transform the array into a permutation of {0,1,…,N‑1}. The cost of an operation is 1 per unit change, therefore the total number of operations equals the sum of absolute differences between the original values and the chosen permutation. For two sequences, the sum of absolute differences is minimised by sorting both sequences and pairing the i‑th smallest elements. Hence we sort the given array and pair it with the sorted target sequence 0,1,…,N‑1. The answer is Σ|sortedA[i] – i| for i = 0 … N‑1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting; overall O(ΣN log N) which fits the limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array (the sort can be done in‑place).

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        arr.sort()
        ans = sum(abs(arr[i] - i) for i in range(N))
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
